name: Harry Edison
abstract: Harry "Sweets" Edison (October 10, 1915 – July 27, 1999) was an American jazz trumpeter and a member of the Count Basie Orchestra. His most important contribution was as a Hollywood studio musician, whose muted trumpet can be heard backing singers, most notably Frank Sinatra.

quotes.text: It was special to all of us. Because Carnegie Hall, gee whiz, that’s where you go to hear Marian Anderson, and hear La Traviata, and Leonard Bernstein. That’s the epitome of music, going to Carnegie Hall. That’s another echelon, that music.
quotes.caption: Edison recalls Carnegie Hall as the epitome of music
quotes.doc_title: Harry Edison — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Junior Mance Part I
about_quotes.evidence: I’ve been working with another group called Lionel Hampton and the Golden Men of Jazz... usually it’s Al Grey on trombone, Clark Terry and Harry “Sweets” Edison on trumpets
about_quotes.doc_title: Junior Mance Part I interviewed by Monk Rowe and Michael Woods, New York City, New York, July 27, 1995 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Joe Ascione
about_quotes.evidence: So I remember getting to meet him and then getting to play with him
about_quotes.doc_title: Joe Ascione interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clearwater Beach, Florida, March 17, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Gary Smulyan
about_quotes.evidence: And there was a band called the Philip Morris Superband... It was Ray Brown and Jeff Hamilton played the first year on drums, and James Moody and Herb Ellis and Urbie Green and Johnny Coles and Sweets Edison and Jerry Dodgion. I mean it was an amazing band. And we went on the road for three months.

about_quotes.speaker: Young Snooky
about_quotes.evidence: Buck Clayton and Harry Edison, you know what I‘m saying. So but I‘m a first trumpet player, so they wanted me in there anyway. So I went on the road with them for about a month till Buck Clayton got well and came back.
about_quotes.doc_title: Young Snooky — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Kenny Barron
about_quotes.evidence: We would be their rhythm section. So we said, maybe we should just get a horn player and have a quartet. ... It was Sweets [Harry Edison] and Bobby Durham, Ray Brown. So it was a lot of fun.
about_quotes.doc_title: Kenny Barron — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Freddie Green
about_quotes.evidence: Sweets and Lester, and Buck... Buck and I used to be roommates, and then Lester and I, and Sweets and I... We got along, together, you know. As a family, as brothers.
